2001-02-01.title: New President Inherits a Paralyzed Congo
2001-02-01.abstract: Control over Congo since assassination of Pres Laurent Kabila slips further into hands of Congo's neighbors and hard-line inner circle that surrounded late president and now surrounds his politically inexperienced son, Pres Joseph Kabila; unless new president can somehow assert authority over his handlers and negotiate with rebels who have already captured half of his country, war there will continue in stalemate; Kabila will arrive in Washington on Feb 1 for meeting with Secretary of State Colin L Powell; photo (M)

2001-02-01.title: Barak Goes for Broke in Israeli Election
2001-02-01.abstract: TEL AVIV, Friday, Feb. 2 — The deadline for political parties to change candidates expired quietly at midnight on Thursday, and Prime Minister Ehud Barak finally put to rest the speculation that he would step aside and let former Prime Minister Shimon Peres run in his place in Tuesday's election.

2001-02-01.title: Homeland Sees Political Motive in Guilty Verdict
2001-02-01.abstract: Libyan television broadcasts rare live report of split verdict in Lockerbie trial, telling confounded nation that one of its own had been found guilty of bombing Pan Am jet; most common reaction among Libyans is nagging suspicion that trial was fixed; not all Libyans seem convinced that outcome is momentous; photo (M)

2001-02-01.title: Taiwan Legislature Reverses Leader on Contested Nuclear Plant
2001-02-01.abstract: Taiwan's legislature votes to reverse decision by Pres Chen Shui-bian to scrap partly built nuclear plant; plant is proxy for much broader battle between Chen and Nationalist Party, which he swept out of power last March after 55 years of rule; legislature, dominated by Nationalists, says halting project has caused economic slowdown and social instability; Prime Min Chang Chun-hsiung says government is not obliged to follow vote; standoff has scared away foreign investors, hobbled stock market and undermined currency (M)

2001-02-01.title: Betty Kenward, 94, Snobbish Chronicler, Dies
2001-02-01.abstract: Betty Kenward, chronicler of England's upper classes, dies at age 94; photo (M)

2001-02-02.title: After Quake, Dearth of Order but Not of Supplies
2001-02-02.abstract: Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee defends Indian government's efforts in response to devastating earthquake; concedes there was some lag in making arrangements and providing immediate relief, but denies there was any confusion, as newspaper editorials have charged (M)

2001-02-02.title: In Test of Troubled Waters, Powell Rebuffs Montenegrin
2001-02-02.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L Powell refuses to see Milo Djukanovic, president of Montenegro, sending firm signal that Bush administration opposes independence for Serbia's last partner in Yugoslav federation; Djukanovic is in Washington to explain why he thinks Montenegro should be independent; Powell's predecessor, Madeleine K Albright, met with Djukanovic several times, although she never explicitly came out in favor of independence for Montenegro; Montenegrins themselves are equally divided over matter of independence (M)

2001-02-02.title: Firgi Journal; Winds of Militant Islam Disrupt Fragile Frontiers
2001-02-02.abstract: Politicians have dangerously widened divisions between Muslims and Christians in West Africa's two most economically and politically important countries, Nigeria and Ivory Coast; now their religious problems have begun to spill over to neighboring countries, and they risk further unsettling already fragile region; spread of Islamic law in Nigeria has spurred militants in southern Niger; Ivory Coast's Christian leaders have strained country's relations with its mostly Muslim neighbors, Burkina Faso and Mali; photo; map (M)

2001-02-02.title: Russian Wants Dialogue With U.S. on Limited Missile Defenses
2001-02-02.abstract: Russian Foreign Min Igor S Ivanov says his country and United States should discuss development of defenses against ballistic missiles in troubled parts of world, but he reiterates Moscow's opposition to any plan to erect national missile defense over United States in violation of 1972 Antiballistic Missile Treaty; Bush administration, like Clinton administration, advocates going forward with national antimissile system, as well as theater antimissile systems intended to protect American troops and bases in conflicts abroad (M)

2001-02-02.title: Videotapes Expose Misdeeds of Peru´s Most Powerful
2001-02-02.abstract: LIMA, Peru, Feb. 2 — They were the most closely guarded secrets of President Alberto K. Fujimori's rule, thousands of videotapes documenting the corruption and sexual high jinks of Peru's most powerful people that he and his spy chief used to blackmail and control virtually the entire political establishment.

2001-02-03.title: Mexico to Extradite an Argentine Accused of Genocide to Spain
2001-02-03.abstract: Mexico will extradite Ricardo Cavallo, former Argentine Navy officer accused of abuses committed under Argentina's dictatorship 20 years ago, to Madrid; in Spain he faces charges of genocide, torture and terrorism against Spaniards; human righs experts say never before has person accused of crimes against humanity been sought for trial in second nation and extradited by third; government of Argentina, which imposed sweeping amnesty protecting junta's soldiers from prosecution, argued against extradition (M)

2001-02-03.title: Clinton Offers Help
2001-02-03.abstract: Former President Bill Clinton has called the Indian prime minister, Atal Behari Vajpayee, to offer help after the earthquake in Gujarat State.     The call, on Thursday evening, was followed by a meeting yesterday in New York between Mr. Clinton and leaders from the local Indian population, including business executives and Indian government officials. Their short-term goal, Mr. Clinton said, was to raise $1 million in relief funds.

2001-02-03.title: A Different Campaign Trail
2001-02-03.abstract: Four days before Israelis vote, Bill Clinton appeared on television and indirectly endorsed the underdog, Prime Minister Ehud Barak, by praising his peacemaking. Page A3.

2001-02-03.title: Clinton, Out, Says What Little He Can to Keep Barak In
2001-02-03.abstract: Former Pres Bill Clinton appears in long Israeli television interview in which he indirectly endorses underdog, Prime Min Ehud Barak, for re-election by praising his courage in peacemaking; high points of interview discussed; photo (M)

2001-02-03.title: Border Agents Reporting Fewer Illegal Mexican Immigrants
2001-02-03.abstract: Number of illegal immigrants from Mexico taken into custody by Border Patrol in Arizona, California and Texas since Jan 1 has dropped by one-third compared with same period last year; law enforcement agents cite greater number of agents posted along border, and say reports of attacks against migrants by ranchers and smugglers may have scared off many prospective immigrants; Mexican officials suggest that election of Vicente Fox, who has pledged to increase economic growth, may be encouraging Mexicans to stay home (M)

2001-02-04.title: Romania, Under Europe´s Scrutiny, Grasps at Stability
2001-02-04.abstract: BUCHAREST, Romania — In the view of its new prime minister and many Western officials and investors who spent the last 10 years watching Romania slide deeper into poverty and despair, the country has one last chance to catch up with Europe and reverse the huge popular frustration that fed a sizable vote for neofascism in last December's elections.

2001-02-04.title: Libyans Stab Themselves To Protest Bomb Verdict
2001-02-04.abstract: Four Libyan students stab themselves in abdomen outside United Nations mission in Tripoli, symbolically proclaiming that they would rather die than acceptsplit verdict from Scottish court over Lockerbie bombing; protesters rail against decision that freed one Libyan intelligence agent but convicted second in 1988 bombing of Pan Am jet that killed 270 people; government-organized protest seems intended to channel some of public outrage and confusion over verdict (M)

2001-02-04.title: Cesare Maltoni, 70, Dies; Cut Workplace Cancer Risks
2001-02-04.abstract: Dr Cesare Maltoni, oncologist who identified cancer risks in workplace, dies at age 70 (M)

2001-02-04.title: French Parties Press for Women in Politics
2001-02-04.abstract: Law passed in France last year appears to go further than any other in world in attempting to share representation more evenly between men and women; law obliges all political parties to field equal number of male and female candidates in almost all elections, starting with municipal elections scheduled in Mar; right now 7 percent of France's mayors and almost 9 percent of its national legislators are women; photos; chart (M)

2001-02-04.title: Hong Kong to Monitor Falun Gong More Closely, Official Says
2001-02-04.abstract: Hong Kong's secretary for security, Regina Ip, says police would more closely monitor Falun Gong's activities, movement that has assumed increasingly high profile in China; Ip's statement comes two days after Beijing warns that it would not tolerate Falun Gong turning Hong Kong into base for anti-Chinese activities (M)

2001-02-05.title: Stagnant Japan: Social Ills Match Economic Ones
2001-02-05.abstract: TOKYO, Feb. 5 — For anyone living in gloomy Japan these days, it is easy to forget that just 12 years ago, this country was seen by the United States and much of the world as a juggernaut, 10 feet tall and rising.

2001-02-05.title: 'People Power II' Doesn't Give Filipinos the Same Glow
2001-02-05.abstract: Filipinos are stung by foreign criticism of popular uprising that brought down Pres Joseph Estrada; fact that Estrada was democratically elected, was expected to survive impeachment trial and fell only after military withdrew its support has cast doubt on dedication of Filipinos to democratic processes; photo (M)

2001-02-05.title: 'Star Wars' and Europe: The Mood Is Shifting
2001-02-05.abstract: European officials are grudgingly accepting fact that Bush administration is determined to move ahead with national missile defense, an issue that they had hoped would just go away; administration has yet to produce a diplomatic strategy for assuring Russia and China that US is not embarking on quest for strategic dominance and Europeans that administration is not embarking on a go-it-alone strategy; photo (M)

2001-02-05.title: Rwandan Leader, in U.S., Urges Push for Peace in Congo
2001-02-05.abstract: Pres Paul Kagame of Rwanda, who is visiting US, warns American and United Nations officials that they must seize opportunity to search for peace settlement in Congo with country's new president, Joseph Kabila, while political situation there remains fluid; describes Kabila as someone who can live in present and look toward future; photo (M)

2001-02-05.title: First Leader to Visit Bush Is Canadian
2001-02-05.abstract: WASHINGTON, Feb. 5 — After spending two weeks getting to know world leaders on the telephone, President Bush experienced his first taste of face-to-face diplomacy today with a White House visit by Prime Minister Jean Chrétien of Canada.

2001-02-06.title: First Leader To Visit Bush Is Canadian
2001-02-06.abstract: Pres George W Bush welcomes Canada's Pres Jean Chretien in first test of face-to-face diplomacy after spending two weeks getting to know world leaders via telephone (S)

2001-02-06.title: India's Extended Families Help Orphans of the Quake
2001-02-06.abstract: Estimated 3,000 children orphaned by massive earthquake in Gujarat State, India, are finding new homes with relatives in keeping with nation's tradition of close family ties and are becoming reminders of those who died and symbols of those who survived (M)

2001-02-06.title: Freud and Einstein On Swiss Bank List
2001-02-06.abstract: Albert Einstein and Sigmund Freud are among holders of 21,000 dormant Swiss bank accounts posted on the Internet in an effort to identify accounts that belonged to Holocaust victims (S)

2001-02-06.title: In Stagnant Japan, Economic and Social Ills Match
2001-02-06.abstract: Article focuses on demise of Japan as economic power over past decade, after being touted little more than 12 years ago as juggernaut and ruthless predator that borrowed American technology and factory methods and would end up dominating virtually every major industry, relegating US to supporting role; with Japan's edge being dulled in industry after industry, either by US or newer rivals in Asia, questions center increasingly on whether country will be able to compete if it fails to look at problems more broadly, addressing not just economic institutions, but its politics and very way Japanese live lives; issues raised in book, Can Japan Compete, written by two Japanese and one American scholar discussed; photo; chart (M)

2001-02-06.title: Lockerbie Verdict Tainted By Politics, Qaddafi Says
2001-02-06.abstract: Libyan leader Muammar el-Qaddafi says guilty verdict in Lockerbie trial, in which Libyan intelligence agent Abdelbaset Ali Mohamed al-Megrahi was convicted of bombing Pan American jumbo jetliner over Soctland in 1988 killing all 270 aboard, is politically tainted and should form basis for removal of economic sanctions against Libya; photo (M)

2001-02-07.title: SHARON EASILY OUSTS BARAK TO BECOME ISRAEL'S PREMIER; CALLS FOR A RECONCILIATION
2001-02-07.abstract: Ariel Sharon, burly 72-year-old hawk who heads Israel's newly reinvigorated right wing, rides wave of national anxiety to overwhelming electoral victory; defeats Ehud Barak 62.5 percent to 37.4 percent, margin unprecedented in Israeli history; highly conciliatory victory speech discusses healing rifts and proposes national unity government; Sharon also reports conversation with Pres Bush, who recalled their trip through West Bank in 1998 when neither would have believed they would be leading their nations now; Barak says he will step down as Labor Party leader and resign his Parliament seat, which casts doubt on chances for unity government; photos; Sharon will otherwise have to build viable coalition in unruly Parliament that was elected with Barak in 1999; new premier calls on 'our Palestinian neighbors to cast off path of violence' and return to dialogue; pledges at same time to keep Jerusalem united under Israeli sovereignty; Yasir Arafat issues statement respecting decision of Israeli people; Labor lawmaker Yael Dayan observes that Barak lost because of 'impossible' situation in which 'we could virtually touch peace,' even as Jews are murdered on roads every day (M)

2001-02-07.title: White House Reaches Out To Both Sides In Peace Talks
2001-02-07.abstract: George W Bush administration, expecting victory of Ariel Sharon in Israel, dispatches messages calling for calm in Middle East and reassuring Israel that relationship with US remains 'rock solid'; Sec Colin Powell issues plea for restraint, call apparently aimed at Palestinian Authority and Arab nations; says he will visit region this month (M)

2001-02-07.title: Taliban Seem to Be Making Good on Opium Ban, U.N. Says
2001-02-07.abstract: Initial results from survey of opium-growing areas of Afghanistan indicate that Taliban may have succeeded in sharply reducing poppy crop; astonished UN narcotics-control officials report no major signs of cultivation this year in northern provinces; farmers tell inspectors they are trying to grow other crops; World Bank warns at same time that country faces major famine; UN appeals for help for 100,000 Afghans caught in bitter cold (S)

2001-02-07.title: Warrior Who Confounds -- Ariel Sharon
2001-02-07.abstract: Man in the News profile of Ariel Sharon, 72-year-old veteran soldier and controversial political figure now elected to lead Israel; photos (M)

2001-02-07.title: Support Mixed With Concern Among U.S. Jewish Groups
2001-02-07.abstract: American Jewish groups react to Ariel Sharon's victory in Israel (S)

2001-02-08.title: Libyan Wants Lockerbie Appeal
2001-02-08.abstract: Abdelbaset Ali Mohmed al-Megrahi tells Scottish courts that he wants to appeal his murder convictions in bombing of Pan American jet over Lockerbie, Scotland, in 1988 (M)

2001-02-08.title: Meeting Today With Rebels Crucial for Colombia's Leader
2001-02-08.abstract: Pres Andres Pastrana of Colombia is set to meet with Manuel Marulanda, who leads Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, and political analysts say that Pastrana risks losing his political legitimacy and presidential authority if he does not get more from Marulanda than a pledge that his group is committed to peaceful resolution of 37-year-old conflict; Marulanda's supporters are divided about whether to continue war or to make peace (M)

2001-02-08.title: Bush Takes First Step to Shrink Arsenal of Nuclear Warheads
2001-02-08.abstract: WASHINGTON, Feb. 8 — President Bush will order a comprehensive review of the nation's nuclear arsenal, a first step toward the unilateral cuts in warheads and missiles that he promised during last year's campaign, senior military and administration officials said today.

2001-02-08.title: Ex-Fugitive Appears at French Corruption Trial
2001-02-08.abstract: Alfred Sirven, former executive at Elf Aquitaine, makes brief appearance in French court, where he is crucial defendant in multimillion-dollar corruption case; is greeted by fellow defendants, including Roland Dumas, former foreign minister, and Dumas's former mistress, Christine Deviers-Joncour; justice minister urges Sirven to tell all he knows about high-level bribery and fraud (M)

2001-02-08.title: Book Calling Holocaust a Shakedown Starts a German Storm
2001-02-08.abstract: Stormy debate is ignited in Germany by publication of The Holocaust Industry, book by Norman Finkelstein, American historian and son of Holocaust survivors, which declares that Holocaust has become an 'extortion racket' through which 'American-Jewish elite' blackmails Germany; Finkelstein says he is unhappy with enthusisastic reception of book by right-wing circles in Germany, but is determined to put 'Holocaust industry' out of business and so end 'exploitation of my parents' memory' (M)

2001-02-09.title: In Unruly Colombia, a Mother's Hope
2001-02-09.abstract: Pres Andres Pastrana of Colombia arrives in heart of guerrilla-held territory to hold first day of meetings with leader of Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, Manuel Marulanda; Pastrana assures mothers and relatives of 470 soldiers and policemen held by FARC that one of first points he will raise with Marulanda is need for exchange of sick rebels held by government for other ailing soldiers held by guerrillas; FARC has said that it would release 50 or more soldiers and policemen once sick prisoners are exchanged; mothers' movement in Colombia has coalesced to put pressure on government; they believe government places priority on securing release of hostages who are wealthy; photos (M)

2001-02-09.title: Russia Vows to Start Destroying Chemical Arms
2001-02-09.abstract: Russia pledges to finally begin destroying 40,000 tons of lethal chemical weapons, largest stockpile in world, this summer; United States and other Western nations are assisting Russia's preparations to destroy its chemical arms, but Congress froze money for project after Moscow missed earlier deadline; funds, up to $50 million a year, can be restored if Moscow puts in place well-financed program (M)

2001-02-09.title: China Is Said to Abuse Promoter of Factory Union
2001-02-09.abstract: Human Rights in China, organization based in New York, says silk-factory worker in China was forcibly placed in psychiatric care in December after trying to organize independent union; says worker, Cao Maobing, is protesting his detention with hunger strike, and that he is being forcibly drugged and subjected to electroshock; Cao's friends and relatives insist he is not mentally ill, and rights group is calling for independent inquiry into his health; Cao has been eloquent spokesman for angry workers at plant in Jiangsu Province (M)

2001-02-09.title: BUSH IN FIRST STEP TO SHRINK ARSENAL OF U.S. WARHEADS
2001-02-09.abstract: Pres Bush will order comprehensive review of nation's nuclear arsenal, first step toward unilateral cuts in warheads and missiles that he promised during his campaign; administration officials say Bush's order will also underscore his commitment to building defense missile shield; say review is intended to move United States toward new strategic doctrine, as well as new approach to arms control that reflects today's world rather than cold war's superpower standoff; say Bush's directive does not declare his intention to withdraw from Anti-Ballistic Missile Treaty; review appears intended to lay foundation for decision to do so in parallel with nuclear cutbacks; nation's arsenal as of last year included 7,519 nuclear warheads on missiles, submarines or bombers; significant reductions in American arsenal could smooth anxieties among opponents about administration's pursuit of missile shield (M)

2001-02-09.title: Hong Kong Will Check on Falun Gong
2001-02-09.abstract: Hong Kong's top official, Tung Chee-hwa, says he is shocked by television images of what Beijing says are members of Falun Gong setting themselves on fire in Tiananmen Square, and that Hong Kong will more closely monitor group, which is outlawed in China but remains legal in Hong Kong; his comments are closely scrutinized, because he has not spoken publicly about Falun Gong since it held conference in Hong Kong at which China's treatment of Falun Gong was protested (M)

2001-02-10.title: Colombia and Rebels Agree on Restarting Peace Talks
POWELL WILL VISIT MIDEAST, SETTING A WIDE ITINERARY
2001-02-10.abstract: State Sec Colin L Powell, in another sign of Bush administration's broad regional approach to Israeli-Palestinian conflict, plans to visit Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Kuwait, Israel and Palestinian territories later in Feb; says he will consult with Israeli Prime Min-elect Ariel Sharon, Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat and other area leaders; also plans to discuss Iraq and ways to keep pressure from United Nations sanctions on Pres Saddam Hussein; Pres Bush says he urged Arafat in phone call to give Sharon chance to form government and fulfill pledge to pursue peace; says he urged calm in talks with Sharon and other regional leaders; Powell's trip will also include stop in Brussels to consult with NATO and European Union leaders on national missile defense system (M)

2001-02-10.title: Sharon Asks Barak and Peres To Join a Unity Government
2001-02-10.abstract: Israeli Prime Min-elect Ariel Sharon asks Prime Min Ehud Barak to join his government as defense minister and Shimon Peres to become foreign minister in bid to form coalition with their Labor Party; Israeli soldiers and Palestinian gunmen exchange fire in both Ramallah and Hebron; Palestinian officials say Israeli soldiers shot Palestinian goatherd to death in Gaza Strip; Islamic Jihad leader Abdallah al-Shami pledges bombing attacks inside Israel will continue, rally, Gaza; Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat speaks by phone to Sharon for first time since election; indicates interest in restarting peace talks; Sharon holds Palestinians must first end violence; photo (M)

2001-02-10.title: Chevron Talks to Azerbaijanis About Pipeline
Europe's Shifting Role Poses Challenge to U.S.
2001-02-10.abstract: BERLIN, Feb. 10 — A little phrase from Rudolf Scharping, the German defense minister, recently caused American military commanders to shudder: "As the European Union develops its security and defense policy and becomes an independent actor, we must determine our security policy with Russia, our biggest neighbor."

2001-02-11.title: Chasing Mexico's Dream Into Squalor
2001-02-11.abstract: Special report The Dividing Line: Misery on the Border describes hardship, squalor and poverty afflicting Mexicans living near United States border, Mexicans who are part of tidal wave of workers lured to border by increased trade between United States and Mexico; in last five years, more than one million Mexicans have moved to border; many come not to cross border, but to work in thousands of mostly foreign-owned manufacturing plants, known as maquiladoras; scene in shantytowns such as Ciudad Juarez described; photos; map (L)

2001-02-11.title: West Lures Its Doctors; South Africa Fights Back
2001-02-11.abstract: South Africa is going public with its battle to stop Canadian government from recruiting its desperately needed doctors, who are fleeing South Africa by the hundreds as they tire of high crime rates and chronically understaffed and underfinanced hospitals; Andre Jaquet, South Africa's ambassador to Canada, argues that it is unethical for the West to lure doctors from the developing world, particularly from South Africa, which has too few doctors (M)

2001-02-11.title: Romania Seeks to Stay On the Track to Europe
2001-02-11.abstract: Adrian Nastase, new Romanian prime minister, knows that his government represents Romania's last, best chance of reversing huge popular frustration that fed a sizable vote for neofascism in last December's elections; Western officials, diplomats and investors know it too, after 10 years of watching Romania slide deeper into poverty and anger; they are skeptical, frustrated by Romania's years of dawdling on economic change, its endemic corruption and narrow-minded political squabbling; photo of Nastase; map (M)

2001-02-11.title: Russia Entrepreneurs Try The Business of Governing
2001-02-11.abstract: As Russia's most powerful businessmen gradually lose their influence over government in Moscow, some have moved into local politics; group of Moscow entrepreneurs has just staged takeover of regional government of Dudinka, far above Arctic Circle in Russia's northernmost region; group is led by Aleksandr Khloponin, 35-year-old former banker; Khloponin's victory follows win by Russia's aluminum tycoon, Roman Abramovich, in region of Chukotka, in Russia's far northeast corner (M)

2001-02-11.title: Shark Tale in Nervous Australia From the One Who Got Away
2001-02-11.abstract: Five Australians have been killed in shark attacks since August, 2000, and a sixth man, Richard Butler, makes dramatic escape after shark takes chunk out of his leg while surfing off Brooms Head; last six months have been bloodiest period of shark attacks ever recorded (M)

2001-02-12.title: Hieroglyphic Script Fights for Life
2001-02-12.abstract: LIJIANG, China—  At his simple wooden desk in a room along the shores of the Black Dragon pool high in the Chinese Himalayas, the brush in He Kai-xian's steady hand marks a series of distinctive symbols, the meaning of which almost nobody else can fully decipher.

2001-02-12.title: Flare-Up of Mideast Violence Continues
2001-02-12.abstract: Flare-up in Israeli-Palestinian violence that followed election of Ariel Sharon as prime minister continues; Palestinian gunmen fire toward Israeli neighborhood of Gilo on outskirts of Jerusalem for first time in more than month; Israeli troops respond with barrage against source of gunfire in Palestinian town of Beit Jala; photo (M)

2001-02-12.title: Ukraine Rally Calls on Chief To Step Down
2001-02-12.abstract: Thousands of demonstrators marched through the capital of Ukraine today, the second such outpouring in a week, demanding the resignation of President Leonid D. Kuchma and calling for new presidential elections.    Up to 5,000 people watched as a wreath of black and white ribbons was laid at the entrance of Ukraine's state intelligence service to celebrate the news that Mr. Kuchma had on Saturday fired its top two officers, Leonid Derkach and Veleriy Strogov. The two had been criticized for orchestrating a crackdown on freedom of the press and suppressing Mr. Kuchma's political opponents.

2001-02-12.title: China's Long — but Uneven — March to Literacy
2001-02-12.abstract: BEIJING—  Hua Lijun owns a most decidedly unfashionable pair of thick black reading glasses, and when she puts them on she has no trouble at all threading a needle. Still, she mutters constantly about her poor eyesight.

2001-02-12.title: Fuel-Short Region in Russia Is Now Freezing in the Dark
2001-02-12.abstract: Russia's Primorsky Region, one of country's most mismanaged and corrupt, is suffering devastating fuel shortage for fourth winter in row in nation that boasts largest coal reserves on earth; regionwide shortage has disrupted lives of hundreds of thousands, brought unspeakable misery for tens of thousands and killed scores; region may be first major test for Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin, who has pledged to bring such wayward areas back under central control; situation in Razdolnoye, desolate town of 13,000 not far from Russia's Pacific coast, described; map; photos (M)

2001-02-13.title: Israel Hunts Down and Kills a Top Arafat Security Officer
2001-02-13.abstract: JERUSALEM, Feb. 13 — Two Israeli helicopter gunships swooped over northern Gaza this morning and dropped four missiles on the car of a senior Palestinian security officer, instantly killing a man whom Israel considered a terrorist in a uniform and a beret.

2001-02-13.title: Second Quake in One Month Kills More Than 100 in El Salvador
2001-02-13.abstract: SAN SALVADOR, Feb. 13 — A powerful earthquake struck El Salvador this morning.

2001-02-13.title: Dozens of Afghans Are Dying in the Cold, U.N. Says
2001-02-13.abstract: United Nations and private aid officials say dozens of Afghan children, exhausted from long journeys with their families to seek food and shelter, have frozen to death at makeshift refugee camps in recent weeks; they are newest, youngest victims of unending Afghan war, and of severe drought, lack of international assistance and shortcomings among ruling Taliban; Bush administration sends mixed signals to Taliban, sending American planes with relief supplies while announcing it would close down Taliban office in New York, which Taliban has used to stay in touch with United Nations officials and with Washington; photo; map (M)

2001-02-13.title: On Africa's Caravan Trail
2001-02-13.abstract: In a corner of Niger that was once a vast sea, dozens of families mine salt, and are the inheritors of a caravan trade that brought life to the Sahara for centuries. Page A12.

2001-02-13.title: China Begins to Shine Light on Use of Torture
2001-02-13.abstract: Instances of police torture in China are increasingly being reported in official press; in rare cases, offending officials are being prosecuted; experts say public reports and prosecutions barely scratch surface of what one Chinese scholar calls legal system's 'chronic illness'; Amnesty International holds torture and ill-treatment of detainees and prisoners is widespread and systematic in China; China is party to United Nations treaty banning torture, but has exempted itself from clause allowing outsiders to investigate alleged torture cases (M)

2001-02-14.title: Israel Hunts Down and Kills a Top Arafat Security Officer
2001-02-14.abstract: Two Israeli helicopter gunships attack car in northern Gaza carrying Massoud Ayyad, killing senior Palestinian security officer Israel deems terrorist; Palestinian officials score Israel's use of assassinations to eliminate Palestinian militants; Prime Min Ehud Barak, other Israeli officials publicly laud mission; Israel's Likud and Labor parties formulate guidelines for unity government based partly on what seems view that peace accord with Palestinians cannot be reached soon; United Nations official Terje Rod-Larsen reports 'anger and uncertainty' have replaced many Palestinians' hopes for peace; finds fighting and Israeli border closings cost Palestinian economy $8.6 million a day; reports urging Israel to bar fiscal collapse of Palestinian authority; photo (M)

2001-02-14.title: Russia Reviving Dreaded Practice of Anonymous Complaints
2001-02-14.abstract: MOSCOW, Feb. 14 — Reviving a practice that was hated, feared and ultimately banned in Soviet times, the domestic successor to the K.G.B. has served notice that it once again is investigating anonymous accusations of crimes against Russian citizens.

2001-02-14.title: German Sees Russia Bending on Missiles
2001-02-14.abstract: German Min Joschka Fischer, meeting with Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin, sees signs of new Russian flexibility on proposed American shield against rogue missiles; he and Russian officials deny Germany acts as intermediary on issue (M)

2001-02-14.title: Palestinian's Hit-and-Run Bus Kills 8 Israelis and Injures 20
2001-02-14.abstract: AZOR, Israel, Feb. 14 -- The day began here with a typical morning rush hour as scores of young Israeli soldiers waited at a junction near Tel Aviv for a ride to their military bases. Pvt. Moshe Saroussi, 19, his black hair spiky and his eyes sleepy, saw an empty bus approaching and stretched, letting the others rush ahead of him.

2001-02-14.title: Civil Suit Over Azerbaijan Oil Deal Gains Plaintiffs in London
2001-02-14.abstract: Columbia University and Pharos Finance Ltd join civil suit in London against Viktor Kozeny, Czech promoter accused of fraud in $450 million deal to acquire control of Socar, Azerbaijan's state-owned oil company; Kozeny holds he did nothing wrong and lost miillions of dollars of his own because Azerbaijan has not sold Socar (M)

2001-02-15.title: EUROPE APPROVES STRICT FOOD RULES
2001-02-15.abstract: European Union Parliament passes measure establishing strict rules on genetically modified organisms, preparing to end Europe's unofficial moratorium on bioengineered seeds and food; strictness of new controls is intended to respond to intense suspicions about genetically modified foods; rules govern testing, planting and sale of crops and food for humans and animals and testing and sale of pharmaceuticals; all genetically altered products will be tracked in database that will also mark locations of all crops; separate bill to set tough food labeling and tracing requirements is to be ready by April, and is widely expected to pass in some form; under European Union law, all 15 member countries are required to make their laws conform to new rules in 18 months; governments of France and Denmark say they would resist approvals; defying laws would open countries to suit in European Court of Justice to force compliance (M)

2001-02-15.title: World Briefing
2001-02-15.abstract: ASIA    AFGHANISTAN: OPPOSITION TAKES CITY -- Bamiyan, a strategic city west of Kabul, has been captured by opposition forces, according to spokesmen for both sides in the conflict. If itcan hold the city, the opposition will deal a major setback to the Taliban militia, which has suffered few battlefield reversals in the past year. Bamiyan was once a bastion for Afghan Shiite Muslims. The Taliban are primarily Sunni.    Barry Bearak (NYT)

2001-02-15.title: Longtime Enemies, Armenia and Turkey Softly Broach Détente
2001-02-15.abstract: Governments of Armenia and Turkey said to initiate quiet diplomatic contacts to evaluate prospects for cooperation and opening border between them; countries have no diplomatic relations, but mid-level officials from both foreign ministries have exchanged visits; talks occur as private organizations in Turkey take steps toward expanding contacts with Armenia and Armenian-Americans; signs of progress are small and come at time of rising anxiety in Turkey over actions by Armenian diaspora to persuade world governments to recognize killing of Armenians under Ottoman Empire as genocide (M)

2001-02-15.title: Transcript of Ambassador Alon Pinkas's Visit to the Middle East Forum
2001-02-15.abstract: Ambassador Alon Pinkas, the consulate general of Israel in New York, answered questions about the Mideast peace process on Wednesday in a forum on The New York Times on the Web.

2001-02-15.title: Profits Raise Pressures On U.S.-Owned Factories In Mexican Border Zone
2001-02-15.abstract: Latest article in Dividing Line series on life along Mexican side of border, on rules, written and unwritten, under which mostly American corporations operate on border; there is rising concern that as factories spread through developing world, labor rights and environmental standards are being overlooked; Mexico's government began offering tax-exempt status to border assembly plants in late 1960's, and over years Mexico and its people came to accept poor working conditions and low pay in return for steady jobs; in Acuna, tensions have flared with Alcoa, which has eight plants there; despite Alcoa's wage increases and improvements in factory conditions, Acuna workers still live in squalid grid of dirt streets, rotting garbage and swamps of open sewage; some officials are beginning to question whether Mexico benefits from tax breaks given to foreign companies; photos; map (L)

2001-02-16.title: Visit by High Indian Minister Warms Ties With Burmese Junta
2001-02-16.abstract: Myanmar, ruled by one of world's most repressive military governments, welcomes high-level Indian cabinet minister, first to visit since Burmese generals set aside 1990 elections and crushed democracy movement; Jaswant Singh, India's minister of external affairs, or foreign minister, speaks during his visit of growing partnership between two nations; Singh tells Indian reporters traveling with him that he hopes India could encourage return to democracy through its engagement with Myanmar (M)

2001-02-16.title: BARAK MAY JOIN SHARON'S CABINET AS DEFENSE CHIEF
2001-02-16.abstract: Ehud Barak has reportedly agreed to serve as defense minister under Israel's Prime Min-elect Ariel Sharon, right-wing leader who crushed him at polls last week; Barak also tentatively agrees to bring his Labor Party into national unity government with Sharon's Likud Party; this would be victory for Sharon's determined bid to forge stable government; agreement between two men is conditioned on final coalition agreement and endorsement of both parties; if agreement stands, Sharon will be flanked by two Laborites, Barak as defense minister and Shimon Peres as foreign minister; Likud-Labor government creates united Israeli front to take on Israeli-Palestinian crisis; Labor would be backing away from or deferring its peace platform, which Sharon opposes; at same time, Labor politicians would be expected to moderate Sharon's hawkish tendencies; photo (M)

2001-02-16.title: Draft Audit Faults Zambia's Mining Deals
2001-02-16.abstract: Confidential auditor's report finds that Zambia had 'imprudent and irregular' dealings in mineral sales that cost country $60 million in lost revenues, just as World Bank and International Monetary Fund were granting debt relief to 22 poor nations, including Zambia; missing revenues are equal to two years of debt relief, in program to encourage sound financial management; many debts date from cold war era of superpower rivalry, when aid was used to build loyalty to West; Zambia case illustrates judgments that Western institutions must make to avoid allegation that by forgiving debts they are helping foster corruption and mismanaged economies; map (M)

2001-02-16.title: U.S. and British Jets Strike Air-Defense Centers in Iraq
2001-02-16.abstract: WASHINGTON, Feb. 16 — The United States and Britain staged air strikes against radar stations and air defense command centers in Iraq today, including targets around Baghdad, in what President Bush called a necessary response to Iraqi provocation.

2001-02-16.title: Congo Leader Offers Talks To the Rebels; Tensions Ease
2001-02-16.abstract: Warring parties in Congo move closer to peace with unexpected agreements on handful of crucial issues; Pres Joseph Kabila, breaking with his late father, who was assassinated, agrees to open political talks with rebels challenging his government; he accepts former president of Botswana, Ketumile Masire, as chief facilitator; in exchange, group challenging Kabila agrees to endorse detailed withdrawal plan that had been signed by other parties in December; photo (M)

2001-02-17.title: Lost Bearings in Israel: Some Expectations Defied
2001-02-17.abstract: News analysis of decision by former Israeli Prime Min Ehud Barak to conditionally accept position of defense minister in Likud government of Prime Minister-elect Ariel Sharon; photo (M)

2001-02-17.title: U.S. AND BRITISH JETS STRIKE AIR-DEFENSE CENTERS IN IRAQ
2001-02-17.abstract: US and British warplanes stage air strikes against Iraqi radar stations and air command centers, including targets around Baghdad, in what Pres Bush calls necessary response to Iraqi provocation; raid, carried out by more than three dozen planes, represents escalation of long-running, low-level skirmishes between American and British jets and Iraqi forces; is first in two years against Iraqi targets north of no-flight zone over southern Iraq, which American and British planes have patrolled since 1992; officials say American military commanders in region requested strikes because Iraqi air defense stations have become increasingly aggressive and effective in targeting patrols over zone; photos; map; diagram (M)

2001-02-17.title: Another Falun Gong Member Reportedly Burns Himself in China
2001-02-17.abstract: Chinese state media report that another member of banned Falun Gong spiritual group has committed suicide by setting himself on fire on street in western part of Beijing (M)

2001-02-17.title: In Spy Hunt, Peru Wants U.S. to Make A Better Effort
2001-02-17.abstract: Peruvian government calls on Bush administration to step up its effort to apprehend Vladimiro Montesinos, fugitive former Peruvian spy chief, saying US has a special moral obligation to find him in view of his long ties to CIA (M)

2001-02-17.title: U.S. Is Set to Assail China on Rights at U.N.
2001-02-17.abstract: Bush administration plans to sponsor United Nations resolution condemning China's record on human rights, but it is unclear how vigorously it will seek international support necessary to make resolution a diplomatic success (M)

2001-02-18.title: China's Crackdown on Sect Stirs Alarm Over Psychiatric Abuse
2001-02-18.abstract: Crackdown on Falun Gong spiritual movement is focusing new attention on China's practice of imprisoning dissenters in psychiatric hospitals; China's official press has openly suggested believers are mentally disturbed and need treatment and hundreds of followers reportedly have been forcibly hospitalized and medicated; photo (M)

2001-02-18.title: AIDS Drug: Giving the Gift of Life in South Africa
2001-02-18.abstract: South African government, after months of criticism for refusing to provide anti-AIDS drugs, has quietly begun offering nevirapine free to pregnant women to help prevent transmission of AIDS virus to infants; clinic in impoverished Guguletu is first in national network of 18 public clinics to offer drug; one tablet taken during labor and single dose for newborn can cut transmission risk by 50 percent; program will reach probably no more than 20 percent of infected pregnant women in AIDS-ravaged nation and offers mothers no hope of drug treatment for selves; map; photo (M)

2001-02-18.title: Bosnian War Trial Focuses on Sex Crimes
2001-02-18.abstract: United Nations war crimes tribunal in Hague hears testimony from many Bosnia Muslim women trapped in 'quasi brothels' in Foca in 1992 by Bosnian Serb soldiers; trial is first to focus entirely on wartime sex crimes and first in international court to prosecute sexual slavery; three former Bosnian Serb fighters on trial, Dragoljub Kunarac, Radomir Kovac and Zoran Vukovic, are charged with mass rape and forced prostitution; photo (M)

2001-02-18.title: Sub Incident Erodes Trust In Japan Chief And the U.S.
2001-02-18.abstract: Japanese are angry at reports that civilian visitors were at controls of United States Navy submarine Greeneville when it collided with Japanese boat Ehime Maru; mounting Japanese irritation over accident is worsening personal and political troubles of Prime Min Yoshiro Mori, who continued to play golf after learning of incident (M)

2001-02-18.title: Death Toll in the Kosovo Bomb Attack on Serbs Rises to 11
2001-02-18.abstract: British peacekeepers and United Nations police say 11 people including baby died in bomb blast, Podujevo, that destroyed bus full of Serbs escorted into Kosovo by Swedish peacekeepers; say two others are missing and dozens hurt; attack raises tensions across Kosovo and sets back return of any Serbian refugees; most Albanians express weariness at violence and shame at terrorism; map; photo (M)

2001-02-19.title: Signs of Uneasiness in Seoul Over Change at White House
2001-02-19.abstract: South Koreans are growing increasingly uneasy about whether they can sustain momentum toward detente with North Korea in face of change of administrations in United States; have been pleased with some of early statements from Bush administration, but note many of Pres Bush's senior advisers have held in recent past that Clinton administration was too eager to normalize ties with North; photo (M)

2001-02-19.title: A Moscow Pooh-Bah, Bearing Up in Brooklyn
Rights Group Tells of Taliban Massacres
2001-02-19.abstract: Human Rights Watch urges United Natio

2001-02-19.title: Warming's Likely Victims
2001-02-19.abstract: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change working group says global warming will likely increase crop yields in temperate northern regions while harming agriculture in tropics, further widening gap between industrialized and poor nations; finds rise in global temperatures in recent decades has already had impact on wildlife, glaciers, sea ice and other earth features; warns of damage and disruption from further temperature rise (M)

2001-02-19.title: But Ministers Tell U.S. And Japan That They Must Stimulate Growth : G-7 Leaders Optimistic On Averting Recession
2001-02-19.abstract: PALERMO, Sicily—  The Group of Seven countries, seeking to soothe fears about the impact of the U.S. slowdown on the global economy, signaled cautious optimism that the world would avert recession this year, but they also made clear that the United States and Japan, the two biggest economies, needed to take steps to stimulate growth and recovery.

2001-02-20.title: Article 2001022090953850769 -- No Title
2001-02-20.abstract: Elisabeth Rosenthal, a New York Times correspondent in Beijing and a physician, visited North Korea Feb. 6-10 with AmeriCares, a private, United States-based relief organization that was inspecting the use of aid it had already provided to North Korea. Two days after Dr. Rosenthal arrived, North Korean authorities told her that she could not work as a reporter during the trip. She said she could not continue the visit under those circumstances, and later left the country.

2001-02-20.title: Barak Turns Down Sharon and Leaves Politics Again
2001-02-20.abstract: JERUSALEM, Feb. 21 — Ehud Barak quit politics for the second time in two weeks early today. He resigned as Labor Party chairman in the face of bitter internal opposition, and declined Prime Minister-elect Ariel Sharon's invitation to serve as defense minister.

2001-02-20.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-02-20.abstract: EUROPE    GERMANY: JEWS TO SUE OVER INTERNET -- The Central Council for Jews said it intended to sue Internet service providers exhibiting extreme right-wing or racist Web pages. The group expressed frustration at what it called the ''snail's pace'' of government action in applying anti-Nazi laws to fight racist and anti-Semitic Internet content.    Desmond Butler (NYT)

2001-02-20.title: Hunting bin Laden's Allies, U.S. Extends Net to Europe
2001-02-20.abstract: Spurred by growing international alarm about Osama bin Laden's militant networks, the police in Britain and Germany have recently arrested more than a dozen Islamic radicals. American officials say some of those arrested were plotting terrorist attacks in Europe and elsewhere.

2001-02-20.title: Emotions Escalate at Hearing on Killings of Israeli Arabs
2001-02-20.abstract: Israel holds first public hearings into police killings of 13 people during wave of violent protests by Israeli Arabs in October; hearings are laden with grief of bereaved families and pent-up grievances of Israel's one million Arab citizens; many Israeli Arabs see police shootings as climax of decades of discrimination, including inadequate government funding for their towns and villages, inferior roads and schools, and unemployment and poverty; protests were in sympathy with Palestinian uprising; photo (M)

2001-02-21.title: Barak, Angry With His Party and Sharon, Quits Politics Again
2001-02-21.abstract: Ehud Barak quits politics for second time since his election defeat, resigning as Israeli Labor Party chairman in face of bitter internal opposition and resigning his seat in Knesset; letter to Prime Min-elect Ariel Sharon declines invitation to be defense minister, accusing Sharon of offering less than wholehearted backing during tumultuous last week; Sharon, without Labor Party, will have shaky right-wing coalition and razor-thin majority (M)

2001-02-21.title: Barak, Under Fire, Again Quits Politics
2001-02-21.abstract: Ehud Barak's resignation from Israeli politics noted

2001-02-21.title: Britain Stops Animal Exports Because of Foot-and-Mouth Disease
2001-02-21.abstract: LONDON, Feb. 21 — Faced with its first outbreak in 20 years of foot-and-mouth disease, a virulent illness that spreads with relentless speed, Britain said today that it was suspending all exports of live animals, milk, meat and other animal products.

2001-02-21.title: Beijing Is Given an Olympian Burnish
2001-02-21.abstract: Chinese authorities in Beijing sweep streets, give taxi drivers crash course in English phrases and generally burnish city in preparation for inspection by technical delegation from International Olympic Committee; photo (M)

2001-02-21.title: Blair Seeking To Ease Tension On Iraq Strikes
2001-02-21.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair, who will be first European leader to visit Pres Bush, offers to revive his Clinton-era role as bridge between US and Europe, even as US and British bombings of Iraqi targets spark tension with European allies; Blair defends actions but indicates he and Bush will discuss revision of sanctions; blames Saddam Hussein's refusal to spend authorized oil revenues on food and medicines for hardships of Iraqi people (S)

2001-02-22.title: U.N. War Crimes Court Convicts Bosnian Serbs in Rape Case
2001-02-22.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, Feb. 22 — In a groundbreaking prosecution of sexual slavery, a United Nations war crimes tribunal in the Hague convicted three Bosnian Serbs for taking part in the systematic rape of Muslim women during the Bosnian war and sentenced the men to long prison terms.

2001-02-22.title: Barak's Party Reels After His Swift Exit
2001-02-22.abstract: Ehud Barak's sudden resignation from political life seems to have cleared way for unity government under Israeli Prime Min-elect Ariel Sharon, despite continued opposition from some in Barak's now leaderless Labor Party; has stilled some of Labor infighting (M)

2001-02-22.title: U.S. Fighter Jet Attacks Iraqi Defenses
2001-02-22.abstract: WASHINGTON, Feb. 22 — An American fighter jet attacked a missile defense battery in Northern Iraq today, Pentagon officials said, resuming low-level skirmishes with Iraqi air-defense forces six days after American and British jets launched the first military strike authorized by President Bush.

2001-02-22.title: Shaping a Legacy, Pope Installs 44 Cardinals
2001-02-22.abstract: Pope John Paul II elevates 44 men to rank of cardinal, largest number ever, rite, Rome; has now named 166 cardinals, more than any other pope, and all but 10 of 135 cardinals eligible to vote for next pope; choses prelates who espouse his traditionalist views and bring greater geographical and ethnic diversity to assembly that will choose his successor; three Americans elevated are Cardinals Edward M Egan, Theodore E McCarrick and Avery Dulles; photos (M)

2001-02-22.title: But Bern Rejects Charges That It Fails to Act Against Money-Laundering : French Assail Swiss on Financial Crime
2001-02-22.abstract: PARIS—  Leftist members of the French Parliament attacked Switzerland on Wednesday over money-laundering, accusing the Swiss authorities of paying only lip service to banking standards intended to fight organized crime.

2001-02-23.title: A Tense Mideast Awaits Powell's Visit
2001-02-23.abstract: JERUSALEM, Feb. 23 — On the eve of General Colin L. Powell's first visit to the region as secretary of state, officials in Israel today rebuffed his pleas for an easing of the blockade of Palestinian territories, while Palestinian militants burned him in effigy.

2001-02-23.title: Desperate Farmers Imperil Peru's Fight on Coca
2001-02-23.abstract: Progress in weaning Peruvian farmers off coca production and into other crops is slowing, and anti-drug officials cannot keep ahead of traffickers and control the many factors that lead farmers to resort to coca in times of economic desperation; coca cultivation actually fell by 12 percent in Peru in 2000, as abandonment and eradication of crop outpaced new plantings; it is feared that as US steps up its effort to eradicate coca crops in Colombia, traffickers will cross borders and increase their activity in Peru; photos (M)

2001-02-23.title: Transcript of President Bush's White House News Conference
2001-02-23.abstract: Transcript of Pres Bush's news conference; photo (M)

2001-02-23.title: New Cardinal In a Clash Of Creeds
2001-02-23.abstract: Cardinal Lubomyr Husar, leader of Ukrainian Greek Catholics, says he wants Russian Orthodox Church and Vatican to realize that Greek Catholic Church is a genuine Eastern church, but his church is viewed with deep distrust by the leading Esatern Orthodox church in Ukraine, which is under jurisdiction of Russian Orthodox patriarch, Aleksy II, and is also in conflict with two rival Orthodox churches in Ukraine; Husar's elevation to cardinal signals Pope John Paul II's desire to support Catholic churches in countries that are predominantly Eastern Orthodox even as he seeks to improve Rome's strained relations with Eastern Orothodoxy (M)

2001-02-23.title: Bush Meets With British Prime Minister
2001-02-23.abstract: WASHINGTON, Feb. 23 — President Bush and Prime Minister Tony Blair of Britain discussed a wide range of world issues today and vowed that their countries would continue to have, as Mr. Bush put it, "an alliance that has made a huge difference in the world."

2001-02-24.title: U.S. and Russians Meet on Defenses Against Missiles
2001-02-24.abstract: CAIRO, Feb. 24 — Secretary of State Colin L. Powell arrived here today on the first leg of his first overseas trip as the United States' chief diplomat and immediately plunged into talks with the Russian foreign minister, Igor S. Ivanov, on the two sides' widely differing plans for missile defenses.

2001-02-24.title: Religious Sect Gains Victory In Moscow Trial
2001-02-24.abstract: Jehovah's Witnesses win potentially far-reaching victory in Moscow court over prosecutors who sought to ban group under 1997 law that prohibits religious sects that incite hatred or intolerance; city court judge throws out charges that Witnesses broke up families, tried to convert minors without parents' consent and even pushed members toward suicide; ruling means group's 10,000 Moscow adherents can continue to practice their religion freely; it also sets political precedent, though not legal one, for many other religious groups outside Russian mainstream; Orthodox Church has been critical of Witnesses, who have been aggressive in recruiting members (M)

2001-02-24.title: Colombian to Ask Bush for Trade Help
2001-02-24.abstract: Pres Andres Pastrana of Colombia says that when he meets with Pres Bush this week he will ask for trade preferences to spur his country's economic growth, rather than press ahead with earlier plan to request $600 million a year in new aid to fight drug trafficking; says he wants Colombia to receive same tariff reductions given to Caribbean and Central American nations through Caribbean Basin Initiative; Colombia is not covered by that trade pact; it is one of four countries in Andean Trade Preference Act, which was intended to stimulate commerce in drug-producing countries but does not offer benefits for important products of Colombia; photo (M)

2001-02-24.title: BUSH TELLS BLAIR HE DOESN'T OPPOSE NEW EUROPE FORCE
2001-02-24.abstract: Pres Bush tells Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain that United States has no objections to separate rapid-reaction military force to help keep peace in Europe as long as it is clearly secondary to NATO; announcement, coming during Blair's visit to Camp David, is notable because both Secretary of State Colin L Powell and Defense Sec Donald H Rumsfeld have indicated reservations about European force, which would make use of NATO equipment and could be dispatched on missions that United States does not endorse, or choses not to join; Blair describes purpose of rapid-reaction force quite narrowly; says it would be limited to peacekeeping and humanitarin tasks and is not a standing army; says it would strengthen NATO by offering alternative when NATO does not want to be involved; photo (M)

2001-02-24.title: Prague Journal; Stalin's Ghost Haunts a Czech Park
2001-02-24.abstract: Aquarium has been proposed for site of huge statue of Stalin in Letna Park in Prague that was destroyed in 1962; photos; map (M)

2001-02-25.title: Pair of Views About Sudan: Help Rebels Or End War
2001-02-25.abstract: Bush administration comes under pressure from Christian groups and members of Congress to help Christian rebels in southern Sudan, but nonpartisan group that includes many diplomatic experts on Africa recommends that United States begin international effort to end 18-year-old war there; conflicting recommendations come at time of increased political turmoil in Sudan itself; Islamist leader, Hassan al-Turabi, who was once allied with Pres Omar Hassan al-Bashir, has been detained, along with 30 of his supporters; Turabi had reportedly undetaken his own efforts to end war, which pits northern Islamic govenrment against southern Christian and animist rebels (M)

2001-02-25.title: Iraq Defiant as U.S. Lobbies Arabs on Shift in Sanctions
2001-02-25.abstract: Bush administration seeks to build international consensus around new, perhaps less punitive sanctions against Iraq; ordinary Iraqis, who have endured decade of incremental misery, are skeptical; country's state-controlled newspapers dismiss Bush's meeting with Prime Min Tony Blair of Britain as 'Anglo-Saxon aggression'; Iraqi officials are defiant, and senior aides to Saddam Hussein are said to feel that Iraq can outlast United States and Britian, using propaganda and diplomacy in Arab world to win complete abandonment of sanctions; photos (M)

2001-02-25.title: Powell Urges an End Violence and Pledges Support
2001-02-25.abstract: RAMALLAH, West Bank, Feb 25v - U.S. Secretary of State Colin Powell urged Israelis and Palestinians on Sunday to end violence that has taken more than 400 lives in five months, but fresh shootings immediately showed the distance left to go.

2001-02-25.title: Swiss Bristle at New Blast at Their Banks
2001-02-25.abstract: GENEVA, Feb. 24 — Swiss bankers and officials are bristling once again over accusations, this time from neighboring France, that the country is an international haven for dubious money dealings.

2001-02-25.title: 10 Years Later, Hussein Is Firmly in Control
2001-02-25.abstract: BAGHDAD, Iraq, Feb. 25 — Ten years ago, Saddam Hussein looked — at least in the view of the American- led military alliance that defeated his forces in Kuwait — every inch a finished man, another discredited dictator heading for the ignominy of history's rubbish heap.

2001-02-26.title: 10 Years Later, Hussein Is Firmly in Control
2001-02-26.abstract: Saddam Hussein is firmly in control of Iraq 10 years after defeat of his forces in Kuwait, when it was thought that his regime would collapse of its own dead weight; he continues to consign opponents, would-be assassins and 'traitors' to prisons, firing squad and gallows; successfully resists any United Nations inspection of his suspected effort to develop chemical, biological and nuclear weapons; Baghdad is rebuilt with glittering new palaces and state guesthouses, imposing new statues of Hussein and scaffolding cladding the few bombed-out government buildings not yet restored; still, a decade of sanctions has reduced Iraqi people, if not pampered elite, to destitution; photos (M)

2001-02-26.title: Swiss Angry At Charges Of Laxness In Banking
2001-02-26.abstract: Swiss bankers and officials are bristling at report to French National Assembly by two Socialist legislators charging that Switzerland is lax in enforcing its laws against laundering money; Finance Ministry accuses authors of arrogance, and Swiss Bankers Association issues point-by-point rebuttal; Swiss banking industry has grown increasingly sensitive to such charges in recent years and believes that it is at disadvantge with banking sectors of competing countries because Swiss laws now require that bankers find out source of money they accept and eschew questionable dealings (M)

2001-02-26.title: Russia to Investigate a Mass Grave in Chechnya
2001-02-26.abstract: Russian military officials promise to investigate grave containing a number of bodies found near Russian military base outside Chechen capital, Grozny, but make clear that they believe that its inhabitants were killed by Chechen rebels and not by Russian soldiers (M)

2001-02-26.title: China Hones Old Tool: 'Re-educating' Unruly
2001-02-26.abstract: BEIJING, Feb. 26 — He had been detained for six months in 1989 for helping to organize a strike, and later he tried to sue the police on behalf of an exiled labor leader, so Zhou Guoqiang, a lawyer and longtime human rights advocate, knew he was on thin ice. Still, he was surprised in 1994 at the pretext they used to jail him.

2001-02-26.title: In India and Africa, Women's Low Status Worsens Their Risk of AIDS
2001-02-26.abstract: Poverty and powerlessness of women in Africa and Asia are combining to make them increasingly vulnerable to AIDS, which some research groups are now calling a women's disease; girls and women in many cultures and in the most disadvantaged societies do not have power to reject unwanted or unsafe sex; more than half the people in Africa infected by AIDS are women; special General Assembly session in June will be devoted to AIDS, and women's groups are increasing their effort to have it focus more attention on women's vulnerability (M)

2001-02-27.title: AMERICAN TOPICS : Getting Religion — Online
2001-02-27.abstract: The Fellowship Church in Grapevine, Texas, is not the only church to establish an Internet site to reach a new generation of parishioners. But its new site goes far beyond the traditional Bible messages or video sales.

2001-02-27.title: The Citibank Connection: Real Money, Shadow Banks
2001-02-27.abstract: Article on Citibank's former ties to mysterious Cayman Islands bank where drug traffickers hid large sums of money; interviews with government investigators and new study of case by Democratic Senate staff raise questions about Citibank's slow response to signs of problems with M A Bank Ltd, even after US agents seized seized $1.8 million in 1998 from Citibank accounts in one of biggest money-laundering probes ever; Citibank continued to do business with shell bank for almost two years, moving another $300 million through accounts before they were finally closed; Citibank and Citigroup deny knowing anything was amiss (M)

2001-02-27.title: Russia Wants to Cut Debt and Raise Influence : With Much to Discuss, Putin Arrives in Seoul
2001-02-27.abstract: SEOUL—  The Russian president, Vladimir Putin, arrived here Monday night in search of an expanded role for Russia on the Korean Peninsula and northeast Asia.

2001-02-27.title: Luxembourg Firm Rejects Allegations
2001-02-27.abstract: PARIS—  A Luxembourg financial facility used by banks to clear stock trades has denounced as "complete nonsense" allegations in France that it has operated secret accounts for money laundering and other illegitimate purposes.

2001-02-27.title: North Koreans Join Relatives In the South As Putin Visits
2001-02-27.abstract: Group of 100 North Koreans arrives in Seoul on third round of long-awaited family reunions; Pres Kim Dae Jung highlights his 'sunshine' policy as he prepares to receive Russian Pres Vladimir Putin (S)

2001-02-28.title: Russia Turns Drug Arrest Into Spy Case
2001-02-28.abstract: Russia's Federal Security Service, domestic successor to KGB, unexpectedly says that American graduate student John Edward Tobbin, arrested nearly month ago on seemingly minor drug charges, was probably working for United States military intelligence; allegations stir tempest in Russian press and apparently surprise US officials, who say they were close to settling drug charges and securing Tobbin's exit from Russia; photo (M)

2001-02-28.title: Sharon Turns To Recruiting Some Partners From the Right
2001-02-28.abstract: Israeli Prime Minister-elect Ariel Sharon, bolstered by Labor Party's agreement to join him in a unity government, shifts his attention to satisfying demands of potential partners from religious and right-wing political factions; Sharon, long a polarizing figure himself, will inherit Parliament that is fractious collection of small and medium-size parties with widely divergent ideologies, and can only assure himself solid majority by taking pragmatic approach of offering government posts to parties to both his left and his right (M)

2001-02-28.title: South Korea Takes Russia's Side in Dispute Over American Plan for Missile Defense
2001-02-28.abstract: South Korean Pres Kim Dae Jung publicly takes Russia's side in debate over United States plan for national missile defense; joint communique issued by Kim with visiting Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin declares that 1972 Antiballistic Missile Treaty, which would be threatend by US plan, is 'cornerstone of strategic stability' (M)

2001-02-28.title: Borneo Backwater's Clashes Draw Little Notice
2001-02-28.abstract: Ethnic clashes that erupted 10 days ago in Kalimantan area of Borneo, where hundreds of people have been decapitated and thousands more left destitute, is drawing minimal response from Indonesian government and little or no reaction by foreign governments; human rights activists and opposition politicians have sharply criticized Pres Abdurrahman Wahid, who departed on two-week overseas trip as conflict unfolded, and Indonesian military and police, which witnesses said stood by and watched as Dayak mobs unleashed campaign of terror against rival Madurese; rejecting calls for his return, Pres Wahid says reports of violence are greatly exaggerated; map; photo (M)

2001-02-28.title: Rwanda and Uganda Cut Back Troops in Congo
2001-02-28.abstract: KIGALI, Rwanda, Feb. 28 — Two armies in the sprawling conflict in Congo took what appeared to be a major step toward peace today, pulling back soldiers from front-line positions with the expectation that other combatants would do the same soon.
